description: These neighborhoods are predominantly Black, with older housing and higher levels of poverty. Many units are renter-occupied, and vacancy rates are elevated, reflecting long-term disinvestment. Households tend to be moderately sized, and commutes are often longer. These areas represent historic core-city communities shaped by decades of uneven development.

These areas are largely White, auto-oriented suburbs with middle incomes and mostly detached homes. Renting is relatively low, and transit use is minimal. The housing stock is moderately old, and communities are stable but not affluent. They reflect classic lower-middle-class suburban environments.

These suburbs are higher-income, well-established, and predominantly White. They combine single-family homes with moderate levels of renting and a more educated population. Neighborhoods are stable, with lower poverty and relatively short commutes. These areas represent upwardly mobile professional suburban life.

These neighborhoods are overwhelmingly Hispanic, with high foreign-born shares and large family households. Detached homes are common, but renting rates remain high. Poverty and education levels vary, but family presence is a defining feature. These areas reflect established Hispanic suburban communities with strong household networks.

These neighborhoods are densely populated with very high shares of immigrants and Hispanic residents. Renting dominates, and public transit is used heavily. Households are large, commutes are long, and poverty is elevated. These areas resemble classic immigrant gateway neighborhoods in older urban cores.

These areas consist largely of parks, airports, and other expansive open spaces with very few housing units or residents. The small number of homes present tend to be extremely high-value, with minimal renting and strong work-from-home patterns. Commutes are the shortest of any cluster, reflecting both low population and proximity to amenities or employment. These tracts represent affluent, ultra-low-density open-space neighborhoods on the edges of urban areas.

These neighborhoods are diverse, well-educated, and dominated by renters. Multifamily housing is common, and transit use is higher than in suburban areas. Incomes are moderate, and household size is smaller. These areas resemble gentrifying or mixed professional urban districts.

These are affluent, highly educated, predominantly White suburban neighborhoods. Detached homes dominate, renting is rare, and poverty is very low. Residents typically commute by car and enjoy high-quality housing. These areas represent upper-tier, stable, and highly desirable suburban communities.
